message.content:
Adversaries may exploit Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM) to interact with remote machines using valid accounts, performing actions as the logged-on user. DCOM extends the Windows Component Object Model (COM) functionality beyond local computers via remote procedure call (RPC) technology. By default, only Administrators can remotely activate and launch COM objects through DCOM, but adversaries with appropriate privileges can remotely execute arbitrary code, including shellcode, through Office applications and other Windows objects with insecure methods. This technique can also execute macros and invoke Dynamic Data Exchange (DDE) directly through a COM instance of a Microsoft Office application, bypassing the need for a malicious document. Tools like Cobalt Strike, Empire, and SILENTTRINITY leverage DCOM for lateral movement by delivering payloads or executing commands remotely.

Potential impacts include unauthorized remote code execution, lateral movement within a network, and execution of malicious payloads. Mitigation strategies involve restricting DCOM permissions, monitoring and auditing DCOM-related activities, enforcing the principle of least privilege, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious DCOM activities. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce vulnerabilities that adversaries might exploit.
